hypothesis: When volatility is high (ATR above a set threshold) a breakout strategy on Keltner bands captures trend moves, while in low volatility the price tends to revert to the Keltner center; switching between these modes with ATR ensures the strategy adapts to regime changes and reduces false signals.

Combining ATR as a volatility regime selector with Keltner band breakouts and ADX as a trend strength filter reduces false entries in both trending and ranging markets; mean‑reversion trades are only taken when volatility is low and ADX indicates a weak trend, thereby improving signal quality and limiting whipsaws.

By using ATR to distinguish high‑volatility and low‑volatility regimes, we apply a breakout rule in the former and a mean‑reversion rule in the latter, while ADX filters out weak trends. This dual‑mode approach reduces false entries in ranging markets and captures trend momentum when volatility spikes, improving Sharpe and reducing drawdown.

When volatility (ATR) is high, the market favors trend following; a price breakout beyond the Keltner upper/lower band with a strong ADX (>25) captures directional moves. When volatility is low, the market tends to revert; a price crossing the Keltner middle band in a weak trend (ADX <20) captures mean‑reversion opportunities. This dual regime logic reduces whipsaws in ranging markets while still exploiting strong trends in volatile periods.

In volatile regimes (ATR above a set threshold) the price tends to break out of the Keltner envelope, while in calm regimes (ATR below the threshold) the price reverts to the envelope's middle line; using ADX to confirm trend strength filters out weak signals and improves entry quality, leading to fewer false breakouts and better risk‑reward balance.

Combining a volatility filter (ATR high) with a trend confirmation (price above EMA) and dynamic price levels (Keltner bands) reduces false breakouts in ranging markets while capturing strong trend moves, improving risk‑adjusted returns.
